sn16.11:1.1: On one occasion Ven. Mahā Kassapa was staying near Rājagaha in the Bamboo Forest, the Squirrels’ Sanctuary.
sn16.11:1.2: And on that occasion Ven. Ānanda was wandering on a wandering tour of the Southern Mountains with a large Saṅgha of monks.
sn16.11:2.1: And at that time approximately thirty of his student monks—mainly youngsters—renounced the training and returned to the lower life.
sn16.11:2.2: Then Ven. Ānanda, having wandered on his wandering tour of the Southern Mountains as long as he wanted, went to the Bamboo Forest, the Squirrels’ Sanctuary near Rājagaha, and went to Ven. Mahā Kassapa. On arrival, having bowed down to Ven. Mahā Kassapa, he sat to one side. As he was sitting there, Ven. Mahā Kassapa said to him,
sn16.11:2.3: “Ānanda my friend, how many motive reasons did the Blessed One have for formulating (the training rule on) three-monk meals among families?”
sn16.11:3.1: “Venerable Kassapa, the Blessed One had three motive reasons for formulating (the training rule on) three-monk meals among families:
sn16.11:3.2: for restraining ill-behaved people and for the comfortable abiding of well-behaved monks; ‘may those of evil wishes, relying on factions, not create a schism in the Saṅgha’; and out of sympathy for families.
sn16.11:3.3: It was for these three motive reasons that the Blessed One formulated (the training rule on) three-monk meals among families.”
sn16.11:4.1: “Then why, Ānanda my friend, are you wandering around with these new monks unguarded in their sense faculties, not knowing moderation in food, and not devoted to wakefulness?
sn16.11:4.2: You wander around destroying crops, as it were.
sn16.11:4.3: You wander around destroying families, as it were.
sn16.11:4.4: Your assembly is falling apart, Ānanda my friend.
sn16.11:4.5: Your following is slipping away, and this youngster doesn’t know his measure.”
sn16.11:5.1: “But, Venerable Kassapa, gray hairs are growing on my head.
sn16.11:5.2: Can’t we escape even today being called a youngster by Venerable Mahā Kassapa?”
sn16.11:5.3: “Ānanda my friend, it’s precisely because you are wandering around with these new monks unguarded in their sense faculties, not knowing moderation in food, and not devoted to wakefulness. You wander around destroying crops, as it were. You wander around destroying families, as it were.
sn16.11:5.4: Your assembly is falling apart, Ānanda my friend.
sn16.11:5.6: and this youngster doesn’t know his measure.”
sn16.11:6.2: “Master Mahā Kassapa has disparaged Master Ānanda, the Vedeha sage, calling him a youngster!”
sn16.11:7.2: “How can Master Mahā Kassapa, formerly the member of another sect, suppose that he can disparage Master Ānanda, the Vedeha sage, calling him a youngster?”
sn16.11:7.3: Ven. Mahā Kassapa heard Thullanandā the nun making this statement, so he said to Ven. Ānanda,
sn16.11:8.1: “Surely, Ānanda my friend, Thullanandā the nun made that statement hastily & without reflecting.
sn16.11:8.3: Ever since shaving off my hair & beard, putting on the ochre robe, and going forth from the household life into homelessness, I don’t recall dedicating myself to any other teacher aside from the Blessed One, the Worthy One, the Rightly Self-awakened One.
sn16.11:8.4: “Before, when I was still a householder, the thought occurred to me:
sn16.11:8.5: ‘Household life is confining, a dusty path. Life gone forth is the open air.
sn16.11:8.6: It isn’t easy, living at home, to practice the holy life totally perfect, totally pure, a polished shell.
sn16.11:8.7: What if I, having shaved off my hair & beard and putting on the ochre robe, were to go forth from the household life into homelessness?’
sn16.11:8.8: “So at a later time, having made4 an outer cloak of patches, having dedicated myself to those who were Worthy Ones in the world, I shaved off my hair & beard, put on the ochre robe, and went forth from the household life into homelessness.
sn16.11:9.1: “Having thus gone forth, I was traveling along a road when I saw the Blessed One sitting by the Bahuputta (ManySon) Shrine between Rājagaha & Nālandā.
sn16.11:9.2: On seeing him, the thought occurred to me,
sn16.11:9.3: ‘If I would see my Teacher, it is this Blessed One I would see!
sn16.11:9.4: If I would see One Well-gone, it is this Blessed One I would see!
sn16.11:9.5: If I would see a Rightly Self-awakened One,
sn16.11:9.6: it is this Blessed One I would see!’
sn16.11:9.7: So prostrating myself right there at the Blessed One’s feet, I said,
sn16.11:9.8: ‘The Blessed One is my teacher! I am the Blessed One’s disciple!
sn16.11:9.9: The Blessed One is my teacher! I am the Blessed One’s disciple!’
sn16.11:9.10: “When this was said, the Blessed One said to me,
sn16.11:9.11: ‘Kassapa, if anyone not knowing were to say, “I know,” or not seeing were to say, “I see,” to a disciple who is so focused with his entire awareness [as you], his head would split open.
sn16.11:9.12: But it’s truly knowing that I say, “I know,” truly seeing that I say, “I see.”
sn16.11:10.2: “A fierce sense of shame & compunction will be established in me with regard to the elders, those newly ordained, and those of middling standing.”
sn16.11:10.3: That’s how you should train yourself.
sn16.11:11.2: “Whatever Dhamma I hear that is connected with what is skillful, I will listen to the Dhamma receptive, attentive, focusing my entire awareness, lending ear.”
sn16.11:11.3: That’s how you should train yourself.
sn16.11:12.2: “I will never forsake my mindfulness immersed in the body connected with joy.”
sn16.11:12.3: That’s how you should train yourself.”
sn16.11:13.1: “Then, having exhorted me with this exhortation, the Blessed One got up from his seat and left.
sn16.11:13.2: For just seven days I ate the almsfood of the countryside as a debtor, but on the eighth day gnosis [the knowledge of arahantship] arose.
sn16.11:14.1: “Then the Blessed One went down from the road and sat at the root of a tree.
sn16.11:14.2: So, having set out my outer robe of patches folded into four, I said to the Blessed One, ‘Lord, may the Blessed One sit here.
sn16.11:14.3: That would be for my long-term welfare & happiness.’
sn16.11:14.4: The Blessed One sat down on the seat laid out.
sn16.11:14.6: ‘It’s soft, Kassapa, your outer robe of patches.’
sn16.11:14.7: “‘May the Blessed One take my outer robe of patches, out of sympathy.’
sn16.11:14.8: “‘But will you wear my robe of cast-off hempen rags?’
sn16.11:14.9: “‘Lord, I will wear the Blessed One’s robe of cast-off hempen rags.’
sn16.11:14.10: “So I offered the Blessed One my outer robe of patches
sn16.11:14.11: and received from him his robe of cast-off hempen rags.
sn16.11:15.1: “Ānanda, my friend, if one were to rightly say,
sn16.11:15.2: ‘a child, a son of the Blessed One, born of his mouth, born of the Dhamma, created by the Dhamma, an heir to the Dhamma, a receiver of his robe of cast-off hempen rags,’ one would be rightly saying it of me:
sn16.11:15.3: ‘a child, a son of the Blessed One, born of his mouth, born of the Dhamma, created by the Dhamma, an heir to the Dhamma, a receiver of his robe of cast-off hempen rags.’
sn16.11:16.1: “Ānanda, my friend, to whatever extent I wish, quite secluded from sensuality, secluded from unskillful qualities, I enter & remain in the first jhāna: rapture & pleasure born of seclusion, accompanied by directed thought & evaluation.
sn16.11:16.2: “To whatever extent I wish, with the stilling of directed thoughts & evaluations, I enter & remain in the second jhāna: rapture & pleasure born of concentration, unification of awareness free from directed thought & evaluation—internal assurance...
sn16.11:17.1: “Through the ending of the effluents—I enter & remain in the effluent-free awareness-release & discernment-release, having directly known & realized them for myself right in the here & now.
sn16.11:17.2: “Ānanda, my friend, one might as well suppose that an elephant seven or seven and a half cubits tall could be concealed with a palm leaf as that my six higher knowledges could be concealed.”
sn16.11:18.1: But Thullanandā the nun fell away from the holy life.
